Now that you have and idea of the what and why, I’ll explain more of the details of what we did.
I’ve broken this section into 3 parts.
By the end of this section you’ll understand how the treatments and how they were applied across the landscape.

Initial harvest: 2012



16 one-hectare blocks

GS: Group selection
LD: Low density dispersed
HA: High density aggregated
HD: High density dispersed


These four treatments were replicated across four sites.












This is how the treatments are defined. They are listed in order from least, to greatest amount of competition (light and growing space).

Here I define the various aspects of the sampling design.
Next, I’ll describe what we sampled in each of these.

Sprout height



25 each of tanoak and redwood sprout clumps were selected for measurement
Tallest sprout in clump measured at years 1, 5, and 10

Regeneration density and composition


We recorded diameter and species of all sprouts and seedlings taller than 1.4 m

Downed woody fuel



Particles were tallied by size class
Counts were converted to load (Mg/ha) using linear intersect theory and parameters from the literature





A transect

Litter and duff



Litter and duff depth measured from a representative location within the sampling cylinder
Depth-to-load equation taken from the literature

Vegetation



We estimated percent cover and average height
Converted to load using a constant bulk density

Sampling review


That was a lot of response variables. Here they are summarized in three categories.

Introduction

By the end of this section, you’ll have an idea of they methods I used to analyze these data.

Analytical Framework

16 models across three response categories

Multilevel (mixed-effects) models throughout

Treatment as primary fixed effect

Random effects reflect study design hierarchy

Model structure selected using AIC + residual diagnostics

Models fit with glmmTMB

Inference based on population-level marginal means



All response variables were analyzed using mixed-effects models to account for nesting within sites, macroplots, and repeated measures where applicable.

Random effects were retained when they explained meaningful variance or influenced fixed-effect estimates.

Candidate distributions and model structures were compared using AIC, and residual diagnostics were evaluated using DHARMa.

Predictions were generated on the response scale and averaged across non-focal predictors to obtain population-level marginal means.

Regeneration Analysis

Basal area (m² ha⁻¹) used to quantify species abundance

Reflects growth + survival under self-thinning

Zero-inflated continuous data → hurdle Gamma model

Treatment as fixed effect

Random intercept: Site × Species



Douglas-fir seedlings analyzed as counts

Overdispersed counts → negative binomial model


Sprout tallies from 4-m radius plots were converted to basal area per hectare using size-class midpoints. Minor species were grouped as “Other,” and absences were coded explicitly as zero.
Basal area was selected instead of stem density because prolific sprouting in redwood and tanoak leads to self-thinning; basal area better captures established biomass rather than transient stem counts.
Basal area was modeled using a hurdle Gamma distribution to separate probability of occurrence from abundance when present. Species-specific dispersion was allowed, and site-by-species random intercepts accounted for hierarchical structure.
Douglas-fir seedlings were analyzed separately using a negative binomial model to account for overdispersion, with random effects for site and macroplot.

Sprout Height Analysis

Two responses:

Height increment (yrs 1–5, 5–10)

Total height at year 10




Normal mixed-effects models




Fixed effects: Treatment, Species, Growth period

Random intercepts: Tree (repeated measures), Macroplot

Species responses allowed to vary within macroplots




Variance modeled as a function of predictors


Height increment included two measurements per tree, so tree was included as a random intercept to account for repeated measures.
Macroplot was included as a random effect to reflect spatial hierarchy.
We allowed species effects to vary within macroplots using an unstructured covariance matrix. This accounts for heteroscedasticity and allows species to co-vary within plots — for example, testing whether plots favorable for one species are also favorable for others.
For height increment, fixed effects included treatment, growth period, species, and the species × growth period interaction.
For total height at year 10, the best model included additive effects of treatment and species.
Dispersion was modeled as a function of predictors to allow variance to differ among treatments, species, and growth periods.

Fuels Analysis

Fuel load (Mg ha⁻¹) estimated by standard planar intercept methods

Classes: 1–1,000 hr, duff/litter, vegetation



Zero-inflated continuous data → Gamma mixed models

Hurdle component used when zeros were present




Random intercepts: Site, Macroplot, Transect corner

Pre- and post-PCT modeled separately

Model components (mean, zeros, dispersion) selected using AIC


Fuel loads were calculated using Brown (1974) methods with regionally derived parameters.
Duff and litter were combined and bulk density was estimated from published regional averages.
Vegetation fuels were calculated using standard bulk density constants and combined to simplify interpretation.
Gamma mixed models with a log link were used because fuel loads are continuous and right-skewed.
A hurdle component was included when zeros were present to separately model occurrence and positive load.
Random effects reflected the nested sampling design (site → macroplot → transect corner).
Pre- and post-PCT fuels were modeled separately because not all fuel classes were measured post-treatment.

What we did
Results

Regeneration density
Sprout height
Fuel

My questions
Your questions

Regeneration Density

No statistically supported treatment differences
Redwood basal area declined sharply with increasing retention

GS highest → HD lowest (~50% reduction per step)

Tanoak response more modest and less variable
Greater uncertainty in redwood responses


Although treatment differences were not statistically significant, trends followed expectations based on light availability.
Redwood showed the clearest decline in basal area with increasing retention, consistent with strong light sensitivity.
Tanoak followed a similar but more moderate pattern, reflecting greater shade tolerance.
Uncertainty was higher for redwood, likely due to spatial variability in light conditions and patchy sprout distribution.

Regeneration Takeaways

Douglas-fir regeneration low (~400 seedlings ha⁻¹) and similar across treatments
Openness most strongly favored redwood
High retention treatments reduced redwood advantage
Trends consistent with shade tolerance theory


Douglas-fir occurred at modest densities and did not differ among treatments.
The primary regeneration signal was the strong response of redwood to openness.
As retention increased, differences between redwood and tanoak narrowed, consistent with shade tolerance expectations.
While basal area differences were not statistically supported, the ecological trends are coherent and align with previous literature.

Regeneration Summary

This figure synthesizes regeneration responses across treatments.
Redwood shows a strong positive response to increasing openness, while tanoak’s response is more moderate.
Under higher retention, differences between species narrow, consistent with shade tolerance theory.
Although uncertainty limited statistical support for basal area differences, the overall directional trends are clear and reinforced by sprout height results, which I will show next.

Sprout height: year 10


Our data were only sufficient to detect a statistical difference for height in year 10 for the GS treatment

Fuel: means



1hr fuels are slightly higher in HD and HA and lower in LD and GS. Likely due to inputs from residual trees.
10– and 100hr fuels are very similar across treatments, but slightly increasing with increasing light.
duff & litter are highest in HD and lowest in HA. Why?
veg is lowest in HA, highest in GS

Fuel: comparisons



1hr HA > GS, and almost HA > LD. Also HD almost > GS
Duff & Litter HD maybe greater than HA
veg GS > HA; LD maybe > HA

Vegetation: Load calc.
We calculated average load for herbaceous and woody vegetation:
\[load = ht. \times pct. cover \times \rho,\]
where \(\rho\) is a bulk density of 8 and 18 Mg/ha/m for herbaceous and shrub components, respectively.

Fuel bed depth


We estimated the combined average fuel bed depth of all litter and downed woody debris within the sampling cylinder.
Regeneration density: frequency
